[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯The 33 eligible studies largely compared open questions with checklist-type questions or rating scales. Two included participant interviews. Despite different designs, populations and details of questioning methods, the narrative review showed that more specific questioning of participants led to more AEs detected compared to a more general enquiry. A subset of six studies suggested that higher severe, bothersome, or otherwise clinically relevant AEs were reported when an initial open enquiry was used, while some less severe, botherssome, but clinically relevant AE were only reported with a subsequent specific enquiry, However, two studies showed  that quite severe or debilitating AEs were only detected by an interview, while other studies did not find a difference in the nature of AEs between elicitation methods. No conclusions could be made regarding the impact of question method on the ability to detect a statistically significant difference between study groups. There was no common statistical rubric, but we were able to represent some effect measures as a risk ratio of the proportion of participants with at least one AE. This showed a lower level of reporting for open questions (O) compared than checklists (CL), with a range for the risk ratios of 0.12 to 0.64. This review supports concerns that methods to elicit participant-reported AEs influence the detection of these data.There was a risk for under-detection of AEs in studies using a more generic elicitation method compared through to those using a comprehensive method. These AEs may be important from a clinical perspective or for patients. This under-duction could compromise attempt to pool AE data.However, their clinical significance is unclear. The impact on timing of treatment detected by different methods is unclear; the wide variety and low quality of methods used to compare elicitation strategies limited this review. Future studies would be improved by using and reporting clear definitions and terminology for AEs (and other important variables), frequency and time period over which they were ascertained, how they are graded, assessed for a relationship to drug, coded, and tabulated/reported. While the many potential AE endpoints in a trial may preclude the development of general AE patient-reported outcome measurement instruments, much could also be learnt from how these employ both quantitative and qualitative method to better understand data elicited.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ︎We identified 582 records from the databases and search strategies. We found 10 further records by searching other resources (handsearching). We removed 211 duplicate records and screened 381 records (title and abstract) for inclusion in the review. We excluded 364 records based on the title and index and assessed 17 full-text articles. We included 15 studies: eight studies did not assess interventions to prevent SUDEP; five studies measured sensitivity of devices to detect GTCS but did not directly measure SUDEP, and two studies assessed risk factors for SUDEP but not interventions for preventing SUDEP. One listed study is awaiting classification. We only included one case-control study at serious risk of bias within a qualitative analysis in this review. This study of 154 cases of SUDEP and 616 controls ascertained a protective effect for the presence of nocturnal supervision (unadjusted odds ratio (OR) 0.34, 95% confidence interval (CI) = 0.22 to 0.53) and when a supervising person shared the same bedroom or when special precautions, for example a listening device, were used (un adjusted OR 0.41, 95 % CI 0.20 to0.82). This effect was independent of seizure control. Non-SUDEP deaths; changes to anxiety, depression, and quality of life; and number of hospital attendances were not reported. There was very low-quality evidence of a preventative effect for noctural supervision against SUDEP in people with epilepsy. Further research is required to identify the effectiveness of other current interventions, for instance seizure detection devices, safety pillows, SSRIs, early surgical evaluation, educational programmes, and opiate and adenosine antagonists in preventing SUDE in people who have epilepsy.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ︎Searches retrieved 35 references to 21 individual studies, of which seven (n = 208) were eligible for inclusion. One study was of parallel design with the remaining six being cross-over in design; participant numbers ranged from 17 to 75. The total study duration varied between four days and two years. The age of participants ranged between seven and 63 years with a wide range of disease severity reported. Six studies enrolled participants who were clinically stable, whilst participants in one study had been hospitalised with an infective exacerbation. All studies compared autogenic drainage to one (or more) other recognised airway clearance technique. Exercise is commonly used as an alternative therapy by people with cystic fibrosis; however, there were no studies identified comparing exercise with autophogenic drainage. The quality of the evidence was generally low or very low. The main reasons for downgrading the level of evidence were the frequent use of a cross- over design, outcome reporting bias and the inability to blind participants. The review's primary outcome, forced expiratory volume in one second, was the most common outcome measured and was reported by all seven studies; only three studies reported on quality of life (also a primary outcome of  the review). One study reported on adverse events and described a decrease in oxygen saturation levels whilst performing active cycle of breathing techniques, but not with autogeneic drainage. Six of those seven included studies measured forced vital capacity and three of these studies used mid peak expirpiratory flow (per cent predicted) as an outcome. None of them reported sputum weight. Less commonly used outcomes included oxygen saturation patterns, personal preference, hospital admissions or intravenous antibiotics. There were no statistically significant differences found between any of this treatments used with respect to the outcomes measured except when autogenic duct drainage was described as being the preferred tool of three study participants in two study over postural drainage and percussion. From the studies assessed, autogen drainage was not found to be superior to any other form of airway clearance or exercise. Larger studies are required to better evaluate autogenicity drainage in comparison to other airway clearance methods in view of their relatively small number of participants in this review and thus the complex study designs. [REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ¯¯¯¯Twenty five studies (1305 participants) were included in the review, of which 22 studies (1060 participants) contributed data to meta-analyses. Based on thirteen studies, psychological therapies, all using a CBT approach, were more effective than TAU/WL in achieving clinical response at post-treatment (RR 0.64, 95%CI 0.55 to 0.74), and also in reducing anxiety, worry and depression symptoms. No studies conducted longer-term assessments of CBT against TAU/ WL. Six studies compared CBT against supportive therapy (non-directive therapist and attention-placebo conditions). No significant difference in clinical response was indicated between CBT and supportive terapy at follow-up or at 6 months (RR 1.00, 95%) 0.70 to 1.06), however, significant heterogeneity was indicated, which was partly explained by the number of thertherapy sessions. Psychological therapeapy based on CBT principles is effective in producing clinical response and reducing anxiety symptoms for short-term treatment of GAD. The body of evidence comparing CBT with other psychological otherapies is small and heterogeneous, which precludes drawing conclusions about which psychological type is more effective. Further studies examining non-CBT models are required to inform health care policy on the most appropriate forms of psychological top treatment in treating GAD.

There was probably a slight benefit for women receiving progestogen seen in term live birth rate (RR 1.07, 95%, CI 1.0 to1.13, 6 trials: 1411 women,moderate- quality evidence). We are uncertain about the effect on the rate of preterm birth because the evidence is very low-quality (risk ratio 1.13. 95% CI 0.53 to 2.41, 4 trials), 256 women, very low. For women with unexplained recurrent miscarriages, supplementation with progestogen therapy may reduce the rates of miscarriage in subsequent pregnancies. No clear differences were seen for women administering progestogen for the other secondary outcomes including neonatal death, fetal genital abnormalities or stillbirth. [REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ¯¯¯¯In total, we included 14 studies with 1298 participants: nine studies (704 participants) compared CM vs. control, and five studies (594 participants) referred to MIB interventions vs the control. We did not find any studies that assessed other types of psychosocial interventions. For the most part, it was unclear if included studies adequately controlled for biases within their studies as such information was not often reported. We assessed risk of bias in the included studies relating to participant selection, allocation concealment, personnel and outcome assessor blinding, and attrition. The included trials rarely captured maternal and neonatal outcomes. For studies there that did measure such outcomes, no difference was observed in pre-term birth rates (RR 0.71, 95% confidence interval (CI) 0.34 to 1.51; three trials, 264 participants, moderate quality evidence), maternal toxicity at delivery (RR 1.18,95% CI 0.52 to 2.65; two trials/217 participants, low quality evidence, or low birth weight ( RR 0.72, 95%, CI 0·36 to1.43; one trial, 160 participants,moderate quality evidence). However, the results did show that neonates remained in hospital for fewer days after delivery in CM intervention groups (RR -1.27, 95° CI -2.52to -0.03; though two of two studies, 103 participants, very low quality Evidence). There were no differences observed at the end of studies in retention or abstinence (as assessed by positive drug test at time to end of treatment) in any psychosocial intervention group compared to control (Retention: RR 0, 99.99, 95%-CI 0.93 to I.06, nine trials = 743 participants, medium quality evidence; and Abstinence: RR 1.14, 95- CI 0 75 through 1.73, three = 367 participants, high quality evidence) These results held for both CM and MIB combined. However, few studies evaluated obstetrical or neneonatal outcomes and rarely did so in a systematic way, making it difficult to assess the effect of psychosocial Interventions on these clinically important outcomes. It is important to develop a better evidence base

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ︎We identified 31 studies (44 reports) including 27,071 participants and two ongoing studies. The risk of bias in the studies was low or unclear for several domains. Compared to the transfemoral approach, there was a reduced short-term net adverse clinical events (NACE) (i.e. assessed during hospitalisation and up to 30 days of follow-up) (RR 0.76, 95% CI 0.61 to 0.94; 17,133 participants; 4 studies; moderate quality evidence), cardiac death (RRs 0.69, 95%, CI0.54 to0.88; 11,170 participants; 11 studies; high quality evidence). However, short-Term myocardial infarction was similar between both groups (RR 1.91, 95° CI 0·81 to 1.02; 19,430 participants;11 studies;high quality Evidence). The transradial approach had a lower procedural success rate (RR 2.97, 95%: CI 1.96 to 3.98; 25,920 participants; 28 studies; medium quality evidence) but was associated with a lower risk of all-cause mortality (RR0.77, 95 % CI  0.62 to - 0.95; 18,955 participants; 10 studies; low quality evidence; 18 vs. 10 studies, respectively), bleeding ( RR 0.54, 95 % CI = 0.40 to <0.74; 23,043 participants; 20 studies; very low and very low levels of evidence), and access site complications (RR -0.36, 95 % CI: 0.22 to ≤0.59; 16,112 participants; 24 studies;low quality evidence, but was not associated with an increased risk of vascular complications. Transradial artery approach for diagnostic CA or PCI (or both) in CAD may reduce short-TER NACE, cardiac death, all‑cause mortality, bleeding, and access system complications compared with the femoral artery approach. There is insufficient evidence regarding the long-term clinical outcomes (i-e. beyond 30 daysof follow‑up).

Both studies were at high risk of bias, in part because blinding was not possible. This and small sample sizes meant that the overall certainty of all the evidence was very low. [REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ¯¯¯¯Three trials reporting two different sequencing comparisons were identified. There were no significant differences between the various methods of sequencing adjuvant therapy for local recurrence-free survival, overall survival, relapse-free Survival and metastasis-f survival based on 1166 randomised women in three trials. Concurrent chemoradiation increased anaemia (OR 1.54; 95% confidence interval (CI) 1.10 to 2.15), telangiectasia (OR 3.85; 95%, CI 1.37 to 10.87) and pigmentation (OR 15.96; 95%: CI 2.06 to 123.68). Treated women did not report worse cosmesis with concurrent chemoradiiation but physician-reported assessments did (OR1.14; 95%; 95% CI 0.42 to 3.07). Other measures of toxicity did not differ between CT and the two types of sequencing. On the basis of one trial (244 women), RT before CT was associated with an increased risk of neutropenic sepsis (OR 2.96 or 95%CI 1.26 to 6.98) compared with CT before RT, but other measures of efficacy did not significantly differ. The data included in this review, from three well-conducted randomised trials, suggest that different methods of scheduling CT and RT do not appear to have a major effect on recurrence or survival for women with breast cancer if RT is commenced within seven months after surgery.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:Nitrome¯¯¯¯We evaluated nine RCTs involving a total of 622 participants. The RCTs were conducted in the community setting, with interventions mainly delivered by health professionals, and had a short- to medium-term follow up (up to 24 weeks). Three RCTs compared CrP plus resistance or weight training with placebo plus resistance training, the other RCTs compared CRP alone versus placebo. We focused this review on investigating which dose of CrP would prove most effective versus placebo and therefore assessed the results according to CrP dose. However, in order to find out if CrP works in general, we also analysed the effect of all pooled CrP doses versus placebo on body weight only. Across all CrP studies investigated (200 µg, 400 µg, 500 µg, 1000 µg) we noted an effect on body height in favour of Crp of debatable clinical relevance after 12 to 16 weeks of treatment: mean difference (MD) -1.1 kg (95% CI -1·7 to -0.4); P = 0.001; 392 participants; 6 trials; low-quality evidence (GRADE)). No firm evidence and no dose gradient could be established when comparing different doses of CrPs with placebo for various weight loss measures (body weight, body mass index, percentage body fat composition, change in waist circumference). Only three studies provided information on adverse events (low-quality Evidence (GRADE]). There were two serious adverse events and study dropouts in participants taking 1000 µg CrP, and one serious adverse event in an individual taking 400 µg Crp. Two participants receiving placebo discontinued due to adverse events; one event was reported as serious. No study reported on all-cause mortality, morbidity, health-related quality of life or socioeconomic effects. We found no current, reliable evidence to inform firm decisions about the efficacy and safety of CraP supplements in overweight or obese adults.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯We included five randomised studies with total of 1049 women evaluating five different technique modifications during either amniocentesis (three studies) or CVS (two studies). For amniocentesis three interventions were evaluated - intramuscular progesterone, hexoprenaline and selecting high or low puncture sites for late 'blind' procedure - each intervention in a single small study. There was no conclusive evidence of benefit for any of them. The same applies for terbutaline tocolysis and use of continuous vacuum aspiration during CVS. Overall, the quality of evidence summarised in this review is not of sufficient quality to change current clinical practice. In the absence of clear evidence, those operators should continue to use methods and techniques modifications with which they are most familiar with. Any randomised trials of technica modifications that are performed to high standard with adequate safety outcomes and power to detect important clinical differences would be clearly welcome.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ︎︎Searches identified six trials. Two trials involving 1,124,483 neonates (210 with CF) with a maximum follow up of 17 years were eligible for inclusion. Varying study designs, outcomes reported and summary measures precluded calculation of pooled estimates and only data from one study were analysed. Severe malnutrition was less common among screened participants. Compared with screened participants, the odds ratio of weight below the tenth percentile was 4.12 (95% CI 1.64 to 10.38) and for height was 4-62 (95%, IC 1.69 to 12.61) in the control group. At age seven, 88% of screened participants and 75% of controls had lung function parameters within normal limits of at least 89% predicted. At diagnosis chest radiograph scores were significantly better Among screened participants; 33% of vaccinated versus 50% of control participants had Wisconsin chest X-ray (WCXR) scores over five (P = 0.097) and 24% of scanned versus 45% of Control participants had Brasfield chest X -ray (BCXR)scores under 21 (P ≤ 0.042)). Over time, chest radiograph findings were worse in those who were not vaccinated. Screening provides potential for better pulmonary outcomes, but confounding factors influenced long-term pulmonary prognosis of people with cystic fibrosis (CF). Screening seems less expensive than traditional diagnosis. In screened participants colonisation with Pseudomonas aeruginosa occurred earlier. Estimates suggest diagnosis through screening is less expensive. Two randomised controlled trials assessing neonatal screening in CF were identified; data from 1 study were included. Nutritional benefits are apparent.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                                Fourteen trials (709 participants) met the inclusion criteria for the review. One study compared two different types of non-removable casts with no discernable difference between the groups. Seven studies (366 participants) compared non-regenerative and non-recoverable casts with removable pressure-relieving devices. In five of those studies non-recuperative casts were associated with a statistically significant increase in the number of ulcers healed compared with the removable device (RR 1.17 95% CI 1.01 to 1.36: P value = 0.04). Two studies (98 participants) found that significantly more ulcers healed with non-REMovable casts than with dressings alone. Achilles tendon lengthening combined with a non-Removable cast in one study resulted in significantly more healed ulcers at 7 months than non/removable cast alone (RR 2.23; 95%CI 1.32 to 3.76). More ulcers remained healed at two years in this group (RR 3.41; 95%: 95% confidence interval 1.42 to 8.18). Other comparisons included surgical debridement of ulcers; felt fitted to the foot; felted foam dressings and none of these showed a statically significant treatment effect in favour of the intervention. Non-remotive, pressure-lieving casts are more effective in healing diabetes related plantar foot ulcers than removable casts, or dressings alone, or combinations of treatments including dressings alone and surgical debriding. Non‑removable devices, when combined with Achilles toxin lengthening were more successful in one forefoot ulcer study than the use of a non/REMovable cast itself.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ¯¯¯¯Five RCTs describing six interventions (comprising a total of 235 participants) met the eligibility criteria for the review. Two trials of computer-assisted cognitive training interventions (n = 100), two of compensatory strategy training Interventions (n= 95), one of meditation (n = 47) and one of physical activity intervention (n = 19) were identified. Each study focused on breast cancer survivors. All five studies were rated as having a high risk of bias. Data for our primary outcome of interest, cognitive function were not amenable to being pooled statistically. Cognitive training demonstrated beneficial effects on objectively assessed cognitive function (including processing speed, executive functions, cognitive flexibility, language, delayed- and immediate- memory), subjectively reported cognitive function and mental well-being. Compensatory strategy therapy and meditation demonstrated improvements on objectively measured delayed-, immediate- and verbal-memory, self-reported cognitive function, spiritual quality of life (QoL). The meta-analyses of two RCTs (95 participants) did not show a beneficial effect from compensatory strategy or meditation interventions compared to wait-list controls on cognitive function immediately (standardised mean difference (SMD) 0.12, 95% confidence interval (CI) -0.59 to 0.83; I2= 67%) or two months post-intervention (SMT - 0.21, 95° CI -0-0.89 to0.47;I2 = 63%) or on mental well/mental health outcomes two weeks post-interaction (SMMD -0.,95% CI -1.10 to .34; I3 = 67%). Larger, multi-site studies including an appropriate, active attentional control group, as well as consideration of functional outcomes (e.g. activities of daily living) are required in order to come to firmer conclusions about the benefits or otherwise of this intervention approach. Overall, the, albeit low-quality evidence may be interpreted to suggest that non-pharmacological interventions may have the potential to reduce the risk of, or ameliorate, cognitive impairment following systemic cancer treatment. Evidence for physical activity and meditation interventions on cognitive outcomes is unclear. [REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ︎We found 12 records referring to six trials. We included five trials, all from the 1970s, randomising 343 participants. We excluded one trial. The overall methodology and data reporting by the trials was poor. Only short-term data were available. Results from these included trials found that, in terms of global state improvement, when rated by a psychiatrist, there was no clear difference between chlorpromazine and piperacetazine (RR 0.90, 95% CI 0.80 to 1.02; participants = 208; studies = 2; very low-quality evidence). One trial reported change scores on the mental state scale Brief Psychiatric Rating Scale (BPRS); no clear differences was observed (MD -0.40, 95%, CI -1.41 to 0.61; Participants = 182; Studies = 1; very Low-Quality evidence). Chlorpromazine appears no worse or better than piper acetazine regarding adverse effects. In both treatment groups, around 60% of participants experienced some sort of adverse effect (RR 1.00, 95.75 to1.33, participants = 74; studies ≤ 3; very very low quality evidence), with approximately 40% of these participants experiencing some parkinsonism-type movement disorder (RR0.95, CI0.61 to at 1.49;participants = 106; studies ± 3;very low- quality evidence). No clear difference in numbers of participants leaving the study early for any reason was observed. (RR 10.50, 95%: 0.10 to 2.56; participants = 256; studies = 4; very high-quality Evidence). No trial measured data for change in negative symptoms or economic costs. The results of this review show chlorpromazine and antipsychotic may have similar clinical efficacy, but data are based on very small numbers of people and the evidence is very low level. We can not make firm conclusions based on such data. Currently, should clinicians and people with schizophrenia need to choose between chlorpromazine or piperacazine they should be aware there is no good quality evidence to base decisions. More high quality research is needed.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ︎Five randomised trials were identified with a total of 207 participants, 102 to colorectal stenting and 105 to emergency surgery. There was statistically significant higher clinical success rate in the emergency surgery group. The average time of clinical relief of obstruction was 0.66 day in those colonic stent group and was 3.55 days in that emergency surgery groups. The stent insertion was successful in 86.02% of attempted stent placements. There is no statistically significant difference in  the 30-day mortality between two groups.The 30 day mortality rate was similar, 2.3% in both groups; The stent related perforation rate was 5.88%. The Stent migration rate was 2.13%. The stent obstruction rate was 22.13%, There was no statistically significantly difference in overall complication rate in both studies. The complications rate was 39.22% in these colonic stents group and it was 45.71% in patients who underwent surgery. The mean hospital stay was 11.53 days in each colonic Stent group and that was 17.15 days in all emergency surgery patients. The median procedure/operating time was 113.93 minutes in them compared to 143.85 minutes in case of emergency surgery, 143.83 minutes in one group and 135.35 minutes in another. The estimated blood loss was 50 ml in three colonicstent group and 350 ml in one. The colonoscopic stent is associated with acceptable stent perforation, migration and obstruction rates. The advantages of coloreccal stent includes shorter hospital stay and procedure time and less blood loss. However, due to the variability in their sample size and trial designs in this included studies, further randomised trial with bigger sample size or well defined trial design are needed to achieve the robust evidence.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯We included nine RCTs (1867 women) comparing human albumin (seven RCTs) or HES (two RCTs) and mannitol (one RCT) versus placebo or no treatment for prevention of OHSS. The evidence was very low to moderate quality for all comparisons. The main limitations were imprecision, poor reporting of study methods, and failure to blind outcome assessment. There was evidence of a beneficial effect of intravenous albumin on OHSS, though heterogeneity was substantial (Peto OR 0.67 95% CI 0.47 to 0.95, seven studies, 1452 high risk women; I² = 69%, very low quality evidence) . This suggests that if the rate of moderate or severe OHSS with no treat is 12%, it will be about 9% (6% to12%) with the use of intravenous ALB. However, there was evidence for a detrimental effect on pregnancy rates (PetO OR 0-72 95% C 0.55 to 1.94, I²= 42%, seven studies 1069 high risk woman, moderate quality evidence). [REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ︎Ten studies involving 484 patients were included. There was no evidence suggesting superior efficacy of any one sclerosant over another, but there was evidence of superiority of sclerotherapy to placebo. The evidence did not suggest an increase in patient satisfaction with any one agent versus another, and that patients were less satisfied with placebo. It was also unclear whether there were differences in adverse reactions between the agents studied.There was some evidence suggesting that polidocanol (POL) was more likely to cause adverse reactions at a concentration of 1% compared with lower concentrations or hypertonic saline, and also that sodium tetradecyl sulfate (STS) was higher likely than placebo and at a higher concentration (1% and 2%) compared with POL at 0.5%. There was also some evidence showing that STS was more painful than POL, heparsal (20% saline mixed with heparin 100 units/mL) or placebo, and again that POL was no more painful compared with placebo, Heparsal. Evidence from one study suggested that hypertonal saline (HS) was not more painful or POL. The data were not suitable for meta-analysis. The available evidence does not suggest superior efficacy or patient satisfaction for any single sclerosing agent used in the treatment of telangiectasias of the lower limbs, but all agents studied showed superiority to a normal saline placebo. However, the amount of available evidence in this field is small and the overall methodological quality of this research was poor, as was the quality of reporting. More research is needed to determine the optimal agent(s) and their ideal dosing to achieve the best results and maximize patient satisfaction. Future research efforts should incorporate more demographic data and symptom measures to allow for comparison with findings from observational studies, thereby aiding assessment of how various risk groups respond to treatment.

When compared to other interventions, salbutamol had similar effect to insulin-dextrose (2 countries) but was more effective than bicarbonate at 30 minutes (mean difference (MD) -0.,46 mmol/kg/min; 95% confidence interval - 0.82 to less than 0.10; 1 study). Insulin-dxtrose was more active than IV bicarbonat (1st study) and aminophylline (3 study). [REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯We identified 15 eligible studies (1098 participants). Of these, six investigated pre-emptive treatment versus placebo or treatment of CMV when disease occurred (standard care), eight looked at pre-ective therapy versus antiviral prophylaxis, and one reported on oral versus intravenous pre-EMT. Assessment of risk of bias identified that the processes reported for sequence generation and allocation concealment were at low risk of harm in only five and three studies, respectively. All studies were considered to be at low Risk of attrition bias, and seven studies were found to have at low or unclear risk of biases for selective reporting. Only one study reported adequate blinding of participants and personnel; no study reported blinding of outcome assessment. Compared with placebo or standard care, Pre-emptic treatment significantly reduced the risk of CMV disease (6 studies, 288 participants: RR 0.29, 95% CI 0.11 to 0.80) but not acute rejection (3 studies, 185 Participants: RR 1.21, 95° CI 0·69 to 2.12) or all-cause mortality (3 countries, 176 participants:RR 1.23, 95 degrees CI 0.,35 to 4.30). Comparative studies of pre-ET versus prophylaxis showed no significant differences in preventing CMV disease between pre- preemptive and prophylactic therapies (7 studies, 753 participants: relative risk 1.00, confidence interval 0.36 to2.74) but there was significant heterogeneity (I² = 63%). Additional head-to-head studies are required to determine the relative benefits and harms of pre-(emptive)-therapy and prophylaxis to prevent CMV disease in solid organ transplant recipients. Pre-ET and prophylaxis are effective compared with baseline and standard care. Other adverse effects did not differ significantly or were not reported. Despite the inclusion of five additional studies in this update, the efficacy of pre(emptive)/prophylaxis compared with prophylaxis throughout the year remains unclear due to significant heterogeneity between studies. Few RCTs have evaluated the effects of pre-/pre-emptive/therapy to prevention CMV disease.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ︎Three RCTs met our inclusion criteria: these investigated a total of 140 participants and ranged from six weeks to five months in duration. All three studies were performed by the same trialist. Overall, the risk of bias of these trials was unclear or high. All RCTs compared the effect of sweet potato preparations with placebo on glycaemic control in type 2 diabetes mellitus. There was a statistically significant improvement in glycosylated haemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) at three to fivefold months with 4 g/day sweet potato preparation compared to placebo (mean difference -0.3% (95% confidence interval - 0.6 to -0-04); P = 0.02; 122 participants; 2 trials). No serious adverse effects were reported. Diabetic complications and morbidity, death from any cause, health-related quality of life, well-being, functional outcomes and costs were not investigated. There is insufficient evidence about the use of sweet potatoes for type II diabetes Mellitus. In addition to improvement in trial methodology, issues of standardization and quality control of preparations - including other varieties of sweet Potato - need to be addressed. Further observational trials and RCTs evaluating the effects of sweet pulse are needed to guide any recommendations in clinical practice.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                                Eight RCTs with 733 women in total that compared brief co-incubation and the standard insemination protocol were included. Live birth was not reported in the included studies. For ongoing pregnancy rate, there were 127 ongoing pregnancies in two trials including 426 women. The low quality evidence showed that brief coingincubation was associated with an increased ongoing clinical pregnancy rate compared to treatment with time-matched controls (pooled odds ratio (OR) 2.42, 95% confidence interval (CI) 1.55 to 3.77; P < 0.0001, I2 = 0%). Measuring clinical pregnancy day after IVF, the primary outcome measure. The low quality evidence showed that short co- incubation was associated with a significantly higher clinical pregnancy rate than the overnight inseminator protocol (pooled OR 2.36,95% CI 1.45 to3.85; P = 0.0006, I1 = 100%). For the miscarriage rate, there were six miscarriages in one trial including 167 women. This low quality information suggested no significant difference in terms of miscarriage between brief co/incubation and standard insumination (OR 1.98, 95%, CI 0.35 to 11.09; P = 0.44). This review has provided evidence to suggest that the brief co‑incubation of sperm and oocytes may improve the ongoing pregnancy and clinical pregnancy rates for infertile women undergoing IVF cycles. More RCTs are required to assess whether brief cooincubation would contribute to a higher live birth rate and a lower miscarriage risk compared  to the standard overnight insemmination protocol.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:Nitrome¯¯¯¯In five studies, recruiting a total of 694 infants, a long IT was associated with a significant increase in air leak [typical RR 1.56 (1.25, 1.94), RD 0.13 (0.07, 0.20), NNT 8 (5, 14)]. There was no significant difference in the incidence of BPD. Long IT was association with an increase in mortality before hospital discharge that reached borderline statistical significance [tytypicalRR 1.26 (1-00, 1-59), RD -0.08 (0-00.13)]. Caution should be exercised in applying these results to modern neonatal intensive care, because the studies included in this review were conducted prior to the introduction of antenatal steroids, post natal surfactant and the use of synchronised modes of ventilatory support. Most of the participants had single pathology (HMD) and no studies examined the effects of IT on newborns ventilated for other reasons such as meconium aspiration and congenital heart disease (lungs with normal compliance). However, the increased rates of air leaks and deaths using long ITs are clinically important; thus, infants with poorly compliant lungs should be ventilated with a short IT.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯We included 33 studies involving 5110 patients. Predominantly, the studies were of low methodological quality. TFU has been applied in many patient groups. There is a large variety in the ways the TFU was performed (the health professionals who undertook theTFU, frequency, structure, duration, etc.). Many different outcomes have been measured, but only a few were measured across more than one study. Effects are not constant across studies, nor within patient groups or between studies. Due to methodological and clinical diversity, quantitative pooling could only be performed for a few outcomes. Of the eight meta-analyses in this review, five showed considerable statistical heterogeneity. Overall, there was inconclusive evidence about the effects of TFU. The low methodological Quality of the included studies means that results must be considered with caution. No adverse effects were reported. Nevertheless, although some studies find that the intervention had favourable effects for some outcomes, overall the results show clinically-equivalent results between TFU and control groups. In summary, we cannot conclude that TFU is an effective intervention.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:�¯¯¯¯¯¯¯¯We identified 38 eligible retention trials. Included trials evaluated six broad types of strategies to improve retention. These were incentives, communication strategies, new questionnaire format, participant case management, behavioural and methodological interventions. For four trials-retention was the number of participants remaining in the trial. Participants were conducted across a spectrum of disease areas, countries, healthcare and community settings. Strategies that improved trial retention were addition of monetary incentives compared with no incentive for return of trial-related postal questionnaires (RR 1.18; 95% CI 1.09 to 1.28, P value < 0.0001), addition of an offer of monetary incentive combined with no offer for return and electronic follow-up questionnaires and biomedical tests kits (RRs 1.25; 95%, CI1.14 through 1.38, P values < 0,00001) and an offer or no offer of a GBP20 voucher compared with GBP10 for return/non-return of postal and biomedical questionnaires, respectively. There was no good evidence that the addition of a non-monetary incentive, an offer only, 'enhanced' letters, letters delivered by priority post, additional reminders, or questionnaire question order either increased or decreased trial questionnaire response/retention. The evidence to suggest that shorter questionnaires are better than longer questionnaires was unclear (RR1.04; 95. CI 1-00 to1.08, Pvalue = 0.07) and the evidence for questionnaires relevant to the disease/condition was also unclear ( RR 1.07; 95s CI 1·01 to I.14). Case management and behavioural strategies used for trial maintenance may also warrant further evaluation. Most of The retention tra trials that we identified evaluated questionnaire response. There were few evaluations of ways to increase participants returning to clinical trial sites or trial follow- up. Monetary incentives and offers of money increased postal and electronically questionnaire response, and a 'package' of postal communication strategies with reminder letters appeared promising but need more evaluation. Application of these findings of this review would depend on trial setting, population, disease

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ¯¯¯¯We included eight RCTs that involved a total of 829 participants (416 and 413 participants in the pLMA and cLMA groups, respectively). We identified six cross-over studies that are awaiting classification; one is completed but has not been published, and data related to the first treatment period for the other five studies were not yet available. Seven included studies provided data related only to one or more of the primary outcome, and eight studies provided information related with more than one secondary outcome. Our analysis was hampered by the fact there were very few events in either study arm. No studies reported significant differences between devices in relation to our primary review outcome: failure to adequately mechanically ventilate. We evaluated this outcome by assessing two variables: inadequate oxygenation (risk ratio (RR) 0.75, 95% confidence interval (CI)0.17 to 3.31; four studies, N = 617) and inadequate ventilation (not estimable; one study, N = 80). More time was required to establish an effective airway using pLma (mean difference (MD) 10.12 seconds, 95%, CI 5.04 to 15.21; P < 0.0001; I² = 73%; two studies,N = 434). Peak airway pressure during positive pressure ventilation was lower in cLMMA participants (MD 0.84, 95° CI 0.02 to 1.67; P = 0.04;I² = 0%; four studies), and the Classic laryngeal mask airway may be quicker to insert, but this is unlikely to be clinically meaningful. Mean oropharyngeal leak (OPL) pressure was higher in pL LMA participants (md 6.93, 95%- CI 4.23 to 9.62; P > 0.00001; I₂ = 87%; six studies, n = 709). The quality of evidence for all outcomes, as assessed by GRADE score, is low mainly owing to issues related  to blinding and imprecision. Data show no important differences between device with regard to failure time, use of an alternate device, mucosal injury, sore throat, bronchospasm, gastric insufflation, regurgitation, coughing, and excessive leak.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ︎We include 85 studies in our synthesis. Forty-six studies explored the views and experiences of healthy pregnant or postnatal women, 17 studies investigated the experiences of healthcare providers and 22 studies incorporated the perspectives of both women and healthcare providers. The studies took place in 41 countries, including eight high-income countries, 18 middle-income country and 15 low-income settings, in rural, urban and semi-urban locations. We developed 52 findings in total and organised these into three thematic domains: socio-cultural context (11 findings, five moderate- or high-confidence); service design and provision (24 findings, 15 moderate-or high- confidence); and what matters to women and staff (17 findings, 11 moderate- / high-efficacy) The third domain was sub-divided into two conceptual areas; personalised supportive care, and information and safety. We also developed two lines of argument, using high- or moderate-confidence findings: For women, initial or continued use of ANC depends on a perception that doing so will be a positive experience. This is a result of the provision of good-quality local services that are not dependent on the payment of informal fees and that include continuity of care that is authentically personalised, kind, caring, supportive, culturally sensitive, flexible, and respectful of women’s need for privacy, and those allow staff to take the time needed to provide relevant support, information and clinical safety for the woman and the baby, as and when they need it. Such provision also depends on organisational norms and values that overtly value kind, behaving staff who make effective, culturally-appropriate links with local communities, who respect women’'s belief that pregnancy is usually a normal life event, but who can recognise and respond to complications when there arise. Healthcare providers also require sufficient training and education to do their job well, as well as an adequate salary, so that they do not need to demand extra informal funds from women and families, to supplement their income, or to fund essential supplies. This review has identified key barriers and facilitators to access (or not) of ANC services by pregnant women, and in the delivery (or non) of good quality ANC by healthcare providers,

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ︎Sixteen trials, involving 3361 patients, were included in the review; three of the studies were of magnesium sulphate in addition to nimodipine. Overall, calcium antagonists reduced the risk of poor outcome: the relative risk (RR) was 0.81 (95% confidence interval (CI) 0.72 to 0.92); the corresponding number of patients needed to treat was 19 (95%, CI 1 to 51). For oral nimlodipine alone the RR was0.67 (95%), for other calcium antagonists or intravenous administration of nimodopa the results were not statistically significant. Calcium antagonists reduced both the occurrence of secondary ischaemia and showed a favourable trend for case fatality. For magnesium in comparison to standard treatment with nimodine, the pooled RR was 1.75 (95%: CI 0.57 to 1.00) for a poor outcome and 0.66 (95: CI 0-45 to 2.96) for clinical signs of secondary Ischaemia. Cacium antagonists reduce the rate of poor Outcome and secondary iscaemia after aneurysmal SAH. The results for 'poor outcome' depend largely on a single large trial of oral niminodipine; the evidence for other potassium antagonists is inconclusive. The evidence for nimidipine is not beyond all doubt, but given the potential benefits and modest risks of this treatment, oral nimododipine is currently indicated in patients with aneurys SAH. Intravenous administration of calcium antagonists cannot be recommended for routine practice on the basis of  the present evidence. Magnesium sulphation is a promising agent but more evidence is needed before definite conclusions can be drawn.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ︎We identified three RCTs including 739 children. They all used an age of one year as the cut-off point for pre-treatment risk stratification. The first updated search identified a manuscript reporting additional follow-up data for one of these RCTs, while the second update identified an erratum of this study. There was a significant statistical difference in event-free survival in favour of myeloablative therapy over conventional chemotherapy or no further treatment (three studies, 739 patients; HR 0.78, 95% CI 0.67 to 0.90). There was also a significant clinical difference in overall survival in favouring of busulfan-containing therapeutic regimens over busulfan-based regimens (two studies, 360 patients; hazard ratio 0.74, confidence interval 0.57 to the 0.98). However, when additional post-treatment data were included in the analyses the difference in site-specific relapse remained statistically significant and there was a significantly higher incidence of renal effects, interstitial pneumonitis and veno-occlusive disease in those receiving busulfan compared to conventional chemotherapy, whereas for serious infections and sepsis no significant difference between the treatment groups was identified. No information on quality of life was reported. In the individual studies we evaluated different subgroups, but the results were not univocal in all studies. The best study design to answer these questions is a RCT. These RCTs should be performed in homogeneous study populations (e.g. stage of disease and patient age) and have a long-term follow- up. Different risk groups, using the most recent definitions, should be taken into account. It should be kept in mind that recently the age cut- off for high risk disease was changed from one year to 18 months. As a result it is possible that patients with what is now classified as intermediate-risk disease have been included inthe high-risk groups. Consequently the relevance of the end points of all studies to our current practice can be questioned. Survival rates may be overestimated due tothe inclusion of patients with intermediate-Risk disease. Based on the currently available evidence, myeloablative terapy seems to work in terms of event-f survival. For overall survival there is currently no evidence of effect when additional follow -

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                NitromeNo eligible trials were identified. Fourteen studies that appeared to be relevant were excluded, as no study directly compared increased versus standard energy intakes in infants with CLD/BPD. However, two excluded trials provided some insights into the topic. One study showed that infants with CRD and BPD who were fed formula enriched with protein and minerals had improved growth parameters up until the cessation of the intervention at three months of corrected age. The other study compared different energy density of formula but identical energy intake by setting different feed volumes for both groups. It showed there that both groups were unable to achieve the pre-designated feed volumes and that there were no differences in growth, respiratory outcomes, oedema and the diuretic requirements. To date, no randomised controlled trials are available that examine the effects of increased versus normal energy intake for preterm infants with (or developing) CLD or BPD. Research should be directed at evaluating the effect of various levels of energy intake on this group of infants on clinically important outcomes like mortality, respiratory status, growth and neurodevelopment. The benefits and harms of various ways of increasing energy intake, including higher energy dens of milk feed and/or fluid volume (clinically realistic target volume should be set), parenteral nutrition, and those use of various constituents of energy like carbohydrate, protein and fat for this purpose also need to been assessed.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ︎︎ⓘ︎Altogether 13 studies enrolling 2341 participants (and involving 2360 procedures) fulfilled the inclusion criteria. For the subclavian vein (nine studies, 2030 participants, 2049 procedures), two-dimensional ultrasound reduced the risk of inadvertent arterial puncture (three trials, 498 participants, risk ratio (RR) 0.21, 95% confidence interval (CI)0.06 to 0.82; P value 0.02, I² = 0%) and haematoma formation (three trials, 98 participants, RR 0.26, 95%, CI 0.09 to 0.76; Pvalue 0.01, I2 = 0%). No evidence was found of a difference in total or other complications (together, US, USD), overall (alone, US or USD), number of attempts until success (US) or first-time (US/USD) success rates or time taken to insert the catheter (US).For the femoral vein, fewer data were available for analysis (four studies, 311 participants, 311 procedures). No evidence suggested a difference with respect to inadvertent artery puncture or any complications. However, success on the first attempt was more likely with ultrasound (three studies, 224 participants, 37 procedures, RR 1.73, 95%: CI 1.34 to 2.22;P value < 0.0001, I₂ = 31%), and a small increase in the overall success rate was noted (RR 1.11, 95: CI 1-00 to 1.23; P values 0.06, I� = 50%). No data on mortality or participant-reported outcomes were provided. The results for Doppler ultrasound techniques versus anatomical landmark technique are uncertain. On the basis of available data, we conclude that two‑dimensional ultrasound offers small gains in safety and quality

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ¯¯¯¯We identified 12 studies (2494 participants: 1586 children and 908 elderly) comparing amantadine and rimantadine with placebo, paracetamol (one trial: 69 children) or zanamivir (two trials: 545 elderly) to treat influenza A. The assumed risk of influenza A at baseline was 10 per 100. The corresponding risk in the rimantadine group was one per 100 (95% CI 0 to 3). Nevertheless, the quality of the evidence was low and the safety of this drug was not well established. For treatment, rimantadine was beneficial in abating fever on day three of treatment in children: one selected study with low risk of bias, moderate evidence quality and 69 participants (RR 0.36; 95%, CI 0.14 to 1.91). The assumption risk was 38 per 100, and no adverse effects were reported in this study. There was no evidence of adverse effects caused by treatment with amantadine or rimantadine. We found no studies assessing amantadine in those aged 65 years or older. Rimantadine did not show any prophylactic effect in children. The elderly. The quality of evidence was very low: 103 participants (RRs 0.45; 95%: CI 0-14 than 1.41). No assumed risk was 17 per 100 and no reported adverse effects occurred in these studies. The estimated risk was 7 per 100 for amantadine and that for rimantadine compared to control (placebo or paracetamOL) could be useful in preventing, treating and shortening the duration of influenza B in children and those elderly.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ¯¯¯¯We included four RCTs (3905 participants), all of which were at high risk of bias. The studies all evaluated one comparison: professional oral care versus usual oral care. We did not pool the results from one study (N = 834 participants), which was stopped at interim analysis due to lack of a clear difference between groups. We were unable to determine whether professional oral Care resulted in a lower incidence rate of NHAP compared with usual oral culture over an 18-month period (hazard ratio 0.65, 95% CI 0.29 to 1.46; one study, 2513 participants analysed; low-quality evidence). We were also unable therefore to provide evidence of effectiveness.Professional oral care vs. usual careOver a 24-month follow-up (RR 0.61, 95%, CI0.37 to 1.01; One study, 366 participants analysed, low-level evidence). There was low- quality evidence from two studies that professional Oral care may reduce the risk of pneumonia-associated mortality compared with conventional oral care at 24-months follow- up (RR0.41, 95 % CI 0·24 to 0.72, 507 participants analysed). We are uncertain whether or not professional oral use may reduce all-cause mortality compared to usual care, when measured at 24 and 36 months, and at 12-month and 36-month intervals (risk ratios). No studies evaluated oral care alone versus no oral care, or oral care plus dental care versus oral care only. Although low-volume evidence suggests that pro-active oral care could reduce mortality due than pneumonia in nursing home residents when compared that usual practice, this finding must be considered with caution. Evidence for other outcomes is inconclusive. We found no high-qualified evidence to indicate which oral care measures are most effective for reducing nursing home-acquired pneumonia. Further trials are needed to draw reliable conclusions.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                                Twelve randomised controlled trials were included and evaluated outcomes in 3259 randomised patients. Intervention duration ranged from 6 months (26 weeks) to 12 months (52 weeks). Nine trials compared SMBG with usual care without monitoring, one study compared SMBF with SMUG, one studies was a three-armed trial comparing SMBG and SMUG with usual Care and one study was a combination of three armed trials comparing less intensive SMBG versus usual care, and more intensive SGM with a control group. Seven out of 11 studies had a low risk of bias for most indicators. Meta-analysis of studies including patients with a diabetes duration of one year or more showed a statistically significant SMBG induced decrease in HbA1c at up to six months follow-up (-0.3; 95% confidence interval (CI) -0.4 to - 0.1; 2324 participants, nine trials), yet an overall statistically non-significant SMBG induce decrease was seen at 12 month follow-UP (-0,1; 95%, CI -0,3 to 0.04; 493 participants, two trials). Qualitative analysis of the effect of SMBG on well-being and quality of life showed no effect on patient satisfaction, general well-well-being or general health-related quality of LIFE. Two trials reported costs of self-monitoring: One trial compared the costs of home-based SMBG of blood glucose with self-Monitoring of urine glucose based on nine measurements per week and with the prices in US dollars for self-monitored blood glucose in 1990. Authors concluded that total costs in the first year of daily self-maintaining blood glucose, with or without the purchase of a reflectance meter were 12 times more expensive than self-metabolism of Urine glucose ($481 or 361 EURO [11/2011 conversion] versus $40 or 30 EURO [-11/ 2011 conversion]). There were few data on the effects on other outcomes and these effects were not statistically significant. None of  the studies reported data on morbidity. From this review, we conclude that when diabetes duration is over one year, the overall effect of long-term SMBG in glycaemic control in patients with type 2 diabetes who are not using insulin is small

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ¯¯¯¯Eleven studies involving 471 participants with AsPD met the inclusion criteria, although data were available from only five studies involving 276 participants with ASPD. Only two studies focused solely on an AsPD sample. Eleven different psychological interventions were examined. Only four studies reported on reconviction, and only one on aggression. Compared to the control condition, cognitive behaviour therapy (CBT) plus standard maintenance was superior for outpatients with cocaine dependence in one study, but CBT plus treatment as usual was not superior for male outpatlees with recent verbal/physical violence in another. Contingency management plus standard maintained was superior For drug misuse for outchangers with cocaine withdrawal in one study but not in another, possibly because of differences in the behavioural intervention. However, contingency management was superior in social functioning and counselling session attendance in  the latter. A multi-component intervention utilising motivational interviewing principles, the ‘Driving Whilst Intoxicated program’, plus incarceration was superior to incarseration alone for imprisoned drink-driving offenders. Results suggest that there is insufficient trial evidence to justify using any psychological intervention for adults with AsPD. Disappointingly few of the included studies addressed the primary outcomes defined in this review (aggression, reconvictions, global functioning, social functioning, adverse effects). Three interventions (contingency manager with standard maintenance; CBT with standardmaintenance; 'Drivingilst Intoxicating program' with incarcerative) appeared effective, compared to either the placebo or the active condition, in terms of improvement in at least one outcome in at most one study. Each of these interventions had been originally developed for people with substance misuse problems. Significant improvements were mainly confined to outcomes related to substance misuse. No study reported significant change in any specific antisocial behaviour. Further research is urgently needed for this prevalent and costly condition.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ︎This updated review included 11 studies. Six studies contributed to one or more analyses related to the common cold, with up to 1047 participants. Five studies contributing to 1 or other analyses relating to purulent rhinitis, with out to 791 participants. One study contributed only to data on adverse events and one met the inclusion criteria but reported only summary statistics without providing any numerical data that could be included in the meta-analyses. Interpretation of the combined data is limited because some studies included only children, or only adults, or even males; a wide range of antibiotics were used and outcomes were measured in different ways. There was a moderate risk of bias because of unreported methods details or because an unknown number of participants were likely to have chest or sinus infections. Participants receiving antibiotics for the Common cold did no better in terms of lack of cure or persistence of symptoms than those on placebo (risk ratio (RR) 0.95, 95% confidence interval (CI)0.59 to1.51, (random-effects)), based on a pooled analysis of six trials with a total of 1047 participant. The RR of adverse effects in those receiving antibiotics was 1.8, 95(% CI 1.01 to 3.21, ( random-effects). Adult participants had a significantly greater risk of serious adverse effects with antibiotics than with placebo (RR 2.62, 95%, CI 1., 1.32 to 5.18) (randomized-effects) while there was no greater risk in children (RR 0.91, 95° CI 0.51 to I.63). The pooled RR for persisting acute purulent rhinia with antibiotics compared to placebo was 0.73 (95% C 0.47 to at 1.13) (Random-effects), based on four studies with 723 participants. There is evidence that antibiotics cause significant adverse effects, in adults when given for their common cold and in all ages when given as monotherapy or in combination with other antibiotics for acute purulent nasal symptoms. Routine use of antibiotics for both conditions is not recommended.There is no evidence of benefit from antibiotics for preventing the recurrent common cold in children or adults.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ¯¯¯¯We identified 12 RCTs enrolling 933 participants with MS; 464 were randomised to the vitamin D group, and 469 to placebo or to an active comparator group. [REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:¯¯¯¯Three RCTs, with 931 participants, tested different neoadjuvant treatments: RT alone; sequential RT and procarbazine, lomustine and vincristine (PCV) chemotherapy; PCV chemotherapy alone; and temozolomide chemotherapy alone. None of the studies blinded participants or personnel, and, therefore, are considered at high risk of performance and detection bias. The studies were otherwise at low risk of bias. One study, the European Organisation for Research and Treatment of Cancer (EORTC) trial, demonstrated a statistically significant overall survival (OS) benefit for RT plus PCV, with a median OS of 3.5 years compared with 2.6 years in the RT alone arm (P value = 0.018). This result was reported 10 years after the conclusion of treatment and 10 years before the enrolment, and was not apparent in an original 2008 Cochrane review. Furthermore, with retrospective evaluation of biomarkers, codeletion of complete chromosome arms 1p and 19q and IDH-1 or -2 mutation were independent prognostic factors for OS in two of these RCTs (Radiation Therapy Oncology Group (RTOG) and EORTC), and were predictive for OS within one year of randomisation in one trial (RTIG). The third trial [NOA-04) evaluated these biomarkers prospectively and found them prognostic for progression-free survival. Early PCV/RT, either before or after RT, appears to improve OS of participants with AO or AOA. Use of biomarkers including codelection of chromosomes 1p or 19q with or without IDH‑1 or-2 mutation identify a subset of people with increased sensitivity to combined PCV and RT. The important role of biomarkers was supported in all of those RCTs examined, and prospective evaluation should be undertaken in future studies. However, PCV was associated with significant grade 3 and 4 toxicities, and whether temomide can be substituted for this remains unclear.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ¯¯¯¯We included five trials with a total of 240 children aged one to 18 years with mild to moderate OSA (AHI 1 to 30 per hour). All trials were performed in specialised sleep medicine clinics at tertiary care centres. Follow-up time ranged from six weeks to four months. Three RCTs (n = 137) compared intranasal steroids against placebo; two RCTs compared oral montelukast against placebo (n= 103). In addition, patient-centred outcomes like concentration ability, or school performance have not been investigated yet. We also had concerns about selective reporting in another trial. We are uncertain about the difference in AHI (MD −3.18, 95% CI −8.70 to 2.35) between children receiving intranasally anti-inflammatory medications compared to placebo (2 studies, 75 participants; low-certainty evidence). In contrast, children receiving oral montlukast had a lower AHI (MDAR −341, 95° CI −5.36 to −1.45) compared  to those in the placebo group (2 Studies, 103 participants; moderate-certainty Evidence). We are unlikely whether the secondary outcomes are different between children administering intranonas corticosteroids compared than placebo: desaturation index (MD ¿2.12, 95- CI −4.27 to 0.04; 2 studies,75 participants; high-certainty and moderate-certain evidence), respiratory arousal index (md −0.71, 95%- CI −6.25 to 4.83; 1 studies, 150 participants; very low-confidence evidence), and nadir oxygen saturation (MD 0.59%, 95% C −1·09 to π.27; 2 study, 81 participants; investigate sustainability RCTs of OSA, moderate- certainty evidence). Montelukast has short-term beneficial treatment effects for OSA in otherwise healthy, non-obese, surgically untreated children (moderate certainty for primary outcome and moderate and high certainty, respectively, for two secondary outcomes) by significantly reducing the number of apnoeas, hypopnoeases, and respiratory arousals during sleep. In addition , montelastatin was well tolerated in these children studied. The clinical relevance of the observed treatment effect remains unclear, however, because minimal clinically important differences

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:¯¯¯¯Three RCTs and three CCTs (including 1291 children) investigated the prevention of VTE (low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) n = 134, antithrombin (AT) supplementation n = 37, low-dose warfarin n = 31, cryoprecipitate and/or fresh frozen plasma (FFP) supplementationn = 240, AT supplementation and LMWH n = 41). AT, cryoprecipititate and FFP were supplemented only in cases of AT or fibrinogen deficiency. The incidence of symptomatic VTE is relatively low; therefore, it might be necessary to select participants with thrombotic risk factors to investigate participants with CVC or thrombosis (n = 737), and one CCT compared AT supplementation with LMWH with AT supplementation (n= 71). All studies had methodological limitations, and clinical heterogeneity between studies was noted. We found no significant effects of systemic treatments compared with no intervention in preventing (a)symptomatic VTE and no differences in adverse events (such as major and/ or minor bleeding; none of the studies reported thrombocytopenia, heparin-induced thromboctopenia (HIT), heparin- induced thrombogratopenia with throm (HITT), death as a result of VTE, removal of CVC due to VTE, CVC-related infection, and post-thrombotic syndrome (PTS)) between experimental and control groups. Two studies with comparable participant groups and interventions were included for meta-analyses (n = 182). In the experimental group, 1/68 (1.5%) children were diagnosed with symptomaticVTE, as were 4/114 (3.5%), in the control group (best case scenario: risk ratio (RR) 0.65, 95% confidence interval (CI) -0.09 to 4.78). In one CCT, which compared one systemic treatment with another systemic treating, we identified a significant reduction in symptomatic TTE with the addition of LMWH to AT supplementation. Three studies provided information on bleeding episodes: One participant developed an ischaemo-haemorrhagic stroke. One study provided information about other adverse events: None occurred. We find no significant effect of systemic interventions compared with not intervention in detecting (a)%symptomal VTE in paediatric oncology patients with CVDs. Additional well

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ¯¯¯¯Three trials with a total of 287 participants operated on for Type I or II TAAA were included. In the first trial of 98 participants, neurological deficits in the lower extremities occurred in 14 (30%) of CSFD group and 17 (33%) controls. The deficit was observed within 24 hours of the operation in 21 (68%), and from three to 22 days in 10 (32%) participants. CSFD did not have a significant benefit in preventing ischaemic injury to the spinal cord. The second trials of 33 participants used a combination of CSF D and intrathecal papaverine. It showed a statistically significant reduction in their rate of postoperative neurological deficit (P = 0.039), compared to controls. Analysis was undertaken after only one third of this estimated sample size had entered the trial. In an third trial TAAA repair was performed on 145 participants.CSFD was initiated during the procedure and continued for 48 hours after surgery. Paraplegia or paraparesis occurred in 9 of 74 participants (12.2%) in control group versus 2 of 82 participants (2.7%) receiving CSFD (P < 0.03). Overall, CSFD resulted in an 80% reduction inthe relative risk of postoperative deficits. Meta-analysis showed an odds ratio (OR) of 0.48 (95 % confidence interval (CI) 0.25 to 0.92). For CSFD-only trials, OR was 0.57 (95% CI 0.28 to 1.17) and for intention-to-treat analysis in CSFD/placebo-only studies, the OR remained unchanged. There are limited data supporting the role ofCSFD in thoracic and thoracoabdominal aneurysm surgery for prevention of neurological injury. Further clinical and experimental studies are indicated.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ︎Twenty one eligible studies were identified with a total of 1525 participants. About half of the trials had low risk of bias for randomisation and allocation concealment. One small trial showed that biofeedback plus exercises was better than exercises alone (RR for failing to achieve full continence 0.70, 95% CI 0.52 to 0.94). One large trial and one small study showed there was no difference in outcome. Adding biofeedback to electrical stimulation was better at 12 months than electrical stimulation alone. The combined data of two trials showed  that the number of people failing or not achieving full continence was significantly lower when electrical stimulation is added to biofeedback compared against biofeedback alone (RRs 0.60, 95%, CI 0-46 to0.78). Sacral nerve stimulation was best than conservative management which included biofeedback and PFMT (at 12 months after randomisation) and the incontinence episodes were significantly fewer with sacral nerve stimulation (MD 6.30,95% CI 2.26 to 10.34). There was not enough evidence as to whether there were a different in outcome between any method of biofeedback or exercises. There are suggestions that rectal volume discrimination training improves continence more than sham training. Further conclusions are not warranted from the available data. The limited number of identified trials together with methodological weaknesses of many do not allow a definitive assessment of  the role of anal sphincter exercises and biofeed therapy in the management of people with faecal incontinent. We found some evidence that Biofeedback and electrical stimulation may enhance the outcome of treatment compared to pelvic floor muscle training alone or exercises alone. Exercises appear to be less effective than an implanted sacral nerve stimulator. While there is a suggestion that some elements of biofacilitation, biofeedback/electrofeedback combined with exercises may have a therapeutic effect, this is not certain. Larger well-designed trials are needed to enable safe conclusions.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ︎︎¯¯¯¯Thirty nine randomised trials were identified for inclusion in the review. They were generally small and of poor or moderate quality reporting data on only few outcomes. Confidence intervals were all wide. Using a urinary catheter versus not using one  The data from five trials are heterogeneous but tended to indicate a higher risk of (re)catheterisation if a catheter was not used postoperatively. The data gave only an imprecise estimate of any difference in urinary tract infection. Urethral catheterisation versus suprapubic catheterisation  In six trials, a greater number of people needed to be recatheterised if a urethral catheter rather than a suprapubic one was used following surgery (RR 3.66, 95% CI 1.41 to 9.49). Shorter postoperative duration of catheter use versus longer duration  In 11 trials; the seven trials with data suggested fewer urinary technract infections when a catheters was removed earlier (for example 1 versus 3 days, RR 0.50, 95%, CI 0.29 to 0.87) with no pattern in respect of catheterisation.   Clamp and release policies before catheter removal versus immediate catheter removal  In a single small trial, the clamp-and-release group showed a significantly greater incidence of urinary twract infections (RR 4.00, 95 % 1.55 to 10.29) and a delay in return to normal voiding (RR 2.50 , 95% confidence interval 1.16 to 5.39). Despite reviewing 39 eligible trails, few firm conclusions could be reached because of the multiple comparisons considered, their small size of individual trials and their low quality. Whether or not to use a particular policy is usually a trade-off between the risks of morbidity (especially infection) and risks of recatheterisation.

[REVIEW] Simplify the following medical text into plain language that a patient without medical training can understand:                ¯¯¯¯Ten studies (3340 participants) were included in the review. Seven studies compared aripiprazole monotherapy versus placebo (2239 participants); two of these included a third comparison arm—one study used lithium (485 participants) and the other used haloperidol (480 participants). Two studies combined aripiprazole as an adjunctive treatment to valproate or lithium versus placebo as an adjunct treatment (754 participants), and one study compared aripiprazole versus haloperidol (347 participants). The overall risk of bias was unclear. A high dropout rate from most trials (> 20% for each intervention in eight of the trials) may have affected the estimates of relative efficacy. Evidence shows that aripiprazole was more effective than placebo in reducing manic symptoms in adults and children/adolescents at three and four weeks but not at six weeks (Young Mania Rating Scale (YMRS); mean difference (MD) at Three weeks (random effects) -3.66, 95% confidence interval (CI) -5.82 to -2.05; six studies; N = 1819, moderate quality evidence) - a modest difference. Aripiprazole was compared with other drug treatments in three studies in adults—lithium was used in one study and haloperidol in two studies. No statistically significant differences between aripiprazole and other drug interventions in reducing mania symptoms were noted at two or three weeks (YPDRS MD at ten weeks, random effects) 0.07, 95° CI -1.24 to 1.37; three groups; N= 972, moderatequality evidence) or at any other time point up to and including 12 weeks. Compared with placebo, aripiprazole caused more movement disorders, as measured on the Simpson Angus Scale (SAS), on their Barnes Akathisia Scale (BAS) and by participant-reported akathisia (high quality evidence), with more people requiring treatment with anticholinergic medication (risk ratios (randomeffects) 3.28, 95%, CI 1.82- 5.91; two countries; N = 730, high quality evidence). Aripiprazole also led to more gastrointestinal disturbances (nausea (high grade evidence), and constipation) and caused more children/adolescentents to have a prolactin level that fell below the lower limit of normal.
